questions.q: How would you secure a public-facing Web API?

answer.oneLiner: Layer it: authenticate and authorize every endpoint, force HTTPS/HSTS, validate all input, lock down CORS, rate-limit, and add security headers.
keyPoints: AuthN + AuthZ on every endpoint (deny by default)
HTTPS + HSTS, input validation, output encoding
CORS whitelist, rate limiting / throttling, WAF in front
Versioning + least-privilege DB and secrets

dialogue.text: We're exposing an internal Web API to the public internet. Where do you start?
I start with the perimeter and identity: force HTTPS/HSTS and make sure every endpoint authenticates and authorizes, deny by default.
Auth is in place. What else hardens it?
Validate all input server-side, lock CORS to a whitelist, add rate limiting, security headers, and put a WAF in front.
Suppose one endpoint is slow and gets hammered by a single client — where do you rate-limit, app or gateway?
Both ideally — coarse limits at the gateway to shed load early, finer per-user limits in the app where I know the identity.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you put rate limiting — app or gateway?
followUpsQA.a: Both: coarse at the edge/gateway, fine-grained per-identity in the app.
A forgotten `[Authorize]` then fails closed instead of leaking an open endpoint.

followUpsQA.q: Is HTTPS enough on its own?
followUpsQA.a: No — it only protects transit; you still need authN/Z, validation, and headers.

followUpsQA.q: What does a WAF add here?
followUpsQA.a: Edge filtering of known patterns (SQLi/XSS/bots) as defense-in-depth.

questions.q: What is CORS and how do you configure it securely in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: CORS is a browser mechanism that controls which origins may call your API via response headers; configure it with a strict origin whitelist, never a wildcard with credentials.
keyPoints: Browser-enforced, not server security — it relaxes same-origin policy
Use `AddCors` + `UseCors` with a named policy
Whitelist specific origins/methods/headers
Never combine `AllowAnyOrigin()` with `AllowCredentials()`

code.note: AllowAnyOrigin + AllowCredentials is invalid and exposes cookies/tokens.

redFlags: Calling CORS a server-side security control

dialogue.text: What problem does CORS actually solve?
It lets a browser relax the same-origin policy so a page from one origin can call your API on another — controlled by response headers.
How do you set it up safely in ASP.NET Core?
`AddCors` with a named policy plus `UseCors`, whitelisting specific origins rather than using a wildcard.
A dev sets `AllowAnyOrigin()` together with `AllowCredentials()` — what happens?
ASP.NET throws — that combo is illegal because it would let any site send the user's cookies; I'd name the exact origins instead.

followUpsQA.q: Does CORS protect the server itself?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's browser-enforced; a non-browser client ignores it entirely.
Wildcard origin + credentials would expose cookies to any site, so the spec forbids it.
Specific origins, methods, and headers — only what the client genuinely needs.

followUpsQA.q: Is CORS a substitute for authorization?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's a relaxation mechanism, not an access control.

questions.q: What is XSS and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: XSS is injecting malicious script into a page so it runs in another user's browser; prevent it with context-aware output encoding and a Content-Security-Policy.
keyPoints: Stored, reflected, and DOM-based variants
Encode output for its context (HTML/attr/JS/URL)
Razor auto-encodes; avoid `Html.Raw` on untrusted data
Add CSP and use HttpOnly cookies to limit impact

dialogue.text: Explain XSS in one breath.
It's getting attacker-controlled script to run in another user's browser in the context of your site.
How do you prevent it in a .NET app?
Context-aware output encoding — Razor auto-encodes — plus a Content-Security-Policy, and I avoid `Html.Raw` on untrusted data.
What about script that never touches the server, built purely in the browser from the URL?
That's DOM-based XSS; I encode/sanitize in the client sink too and avoid `innerHTML` with untrusted data, since server encoding alone won't catch it.

followUpsQA.a: XSS where untrusted data reaches a client-side sink like `innerHTML` without ever round-tripping the server.

followUpsQA.q: How does CSP reduce XSS impact?
followUpsQA.a: It restricts which script sources can run, blocking injected/inline scripts even if encoding is missed.
It stops injected script from reading the session cookie, limiting the damage.
There are countless injection contexts; encoding for the output context is the real fix.

questions.q: What is CSRF and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: CSRF tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending an unwanted authenticated request; prevent it with antiforgery tokens and SameSite cookies.
keyPoints: Only affects cookie/credential-based auth (browser auto-sends)
Use antiforgery tokens (`[ValidateAntiForgeryToken]`)
Set cookies `SameSite=Lax/Strict` + `Secure`
Token-in-header APIs (Bearer) are largely immune

dialogue.text: What is CSRF?
It tricks a logged-in user's browser into firing an authenticated request to your site that the user never intended.
Why does it work, and how do you stop it?
It works because the browser auto-attaches cookies; I stop it with antiforgery tokens and `SameSite=Lax/Strict` cookies.
Our SPA calls the API with a Bearer token in the Authorization header — are we still exposed?
Largely no — the browser doesn't auto-send Authorization headers, so a cross-site request can't forge it; CSRF mainly bites cookie-based auth.

followUpsQA.q: Why are pure JWT-in-header APIs less exposed?
followUpsQA.a: Browsers don't auto-attach Authorization headers cross-site, so the request can't be forged.
Lax allows top-level navigation GETs; Strict blocks all cross-site sends — Strict is safer but can break links.
No — HTTPS protects transit, not the user's intent.
On state-changing requests (POST/PUT/DELETE), validated server-side.

questions.q: How do you prevent SQL injection?

answer.oneLiner: Never concatenate user input into queries — use parameterized queries or an ORM that parameterizes, plus least-privilege DB accounts.
keyPoints: Parameterized commands / EF LINQ are parameterized
Use `FromSqlInterpolated` not string concat in EF
Least-privilege DB user, no `sa`/`db_owner`
Validate input and put a WAF in front as defense-in-depth

code.note: FromSqlRaw with string concatenation is NOT safe.

dialogue.text: How do you prevent SQL injection?
Never concatenate user input into SQL — use parameterized commands, or EF which parameterizes for you.
In EF specifically, what's the safe vs unsafe raw call?
`FromSqlInterpolated` is safe because it parameterizes the interpolation; `FromSqlRaw` with a concatenated string is not.
Say injection still slips through somewhere — what limits the blast radius?
A least-privilege DB account — if the app user can't drop tables or read other schemas, a successful injection does far less damage.

followUpsQA.q: Is EF immune to SQL injection?
followUpsQA.a: LINQ is parameterized, but `FromSqlRaw` with string concat reopens the hole.

followUpsQA.q: What does least privilege add here?
followUpsQA.a: It caps the damage if injection succeeds — no db_owner, only needed CRUD.

followUpsQA.q: Why isn't input validation enough alone?
followUpsQA.a: It's defense-in-depth; parameterization is the actual fix.

followUpsQA.q: Safe way to run raw SQL in EF?
followUpsQA.a: `FromSqlInterpolated` / parameter objects, never concatenated strings.

questions.q: What is over-posting / mass assignment and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: Over-posting is when a client binds extra fields (like `IsAdmin`) you never intended to accept; prevent it by binding to purpose-built DTOs instead of entities.
keyPoints: Happens when model binding maps to your domain entity directly
Use input DTOs/view models with only allowed fields
Or restrict with `[Bind]` / `[BindNever]`
Map DTO to entity explicitly server-side

code.note: Never bind directly to an entity that has privileged fields.

redFlags: Binding requests straight to EF entities

dialogue.text: What is over-posting, or mass assignment?
It's when a client sends extra fields you didn't intend to accept — like `IsAdmin` — and model binding quietly sets them.
How do you prevent it?
Bind to a purpose-built input DTO that only contains allowed fields, then map to the entity explicitly server-side.
A teammate prefers `[Bind]` on the entity instead of a DTO — your take?
It works but it's fragile; a new privileged property silently becomes bindable, whereas a DTO is allow-list by construction.

followUpsQA.a: Marks a property so model binding never populates it from the request.
DTOs are allow-list by design; `[Bind]` lists can drift as the entity grows.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you map DTO to entity?
followUpsQA.a: Explicitly server-side, copying only the intended fields.
Posting `IsAdmin=true` to a profile-update endpoint bound to the entity.

questions.q: Which security response headers matter and what do they do?

answer.oneLiner: A small set of response headers harden the browser: CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy.
keyPoints: `Content-Security-Policy` — restricts script/resource sources (XSS)
`X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` — stops MIME sniffing
`X-Frame-Options: DENY` — anti-clickjacking; `Strict-Transport-Security` — force HTTPS
`Referrer-Policy` + `Permissions-Policy` — limit leakage / browser features

code.note: CSP is the strongest but needs tuning to avoid breaking the app.

dialogue.text: Which security response headers do you reach for first?
CSP, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options`, HSTS, plus Referrer-Policy and Permissions-Policy.
What does CSP buy you over the others?
It's the strongest XSS mitigation — it restricts which script and resource sources the browser will load, but it needs tuning.
You enable HSTS and your team can't reach the site on `localhost` over HTTP anymore — what happened?
HSTS got cached and forces HTTPS even in dev; I keep `UseHsts` for production only and exclude localhost so dev isn't pinned to HTTPS.

followUpsQA.q: Which header replaces X-Frame-Options in modern CSP?
followUpsQA.a: `frame-ancestors` in the Content-Security-Policy.
It hardcodes HTTPS-only into browsers, removing the first insecure request.
The browser guessing content types and executing, say, a script served as text.

followUpsQA.q: Why not ship CSP with unsafe-inline everywhere?
followUpsQA.a: It defeats CSP's main purpose by re-allowing inline injected script.

questions.q: What is clickjacking and how do you defend against it?

answer.oneLiner: Clickjacking tricks a user into clicking on your site loaded invisibly inside an attacker's iframe; block it by refusing to be framed.
keyPoints: Attacker overlays/hides your page in an iframe
Defend with `X-Frame-Options: DENY` or `SAMEORIGIN`
Modern equivalent: CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'`
Use both for broad browser coverage
redFlags: No framing protection on sensitive pages

dialogue.text: What's clickjacking?
An attacker loads your page invisibly in an iframe and overlays it, so the user's clicks hit your site without realizing it.
How do you defend against it?
Refuse to be framed — `X-Frame-Options: DENY` and the modern CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'`.
Your app embeds its own dashboards in its own pages — does DENY break that?
Yes — I'd use `SAMEORIGIN` / `frame-ancestors 'self'` so my own origin can frame it but no one else can.

followUpsQA.a: Same goal; `frame-ancestors` is the modern, more flexible CSP directive — set both for coverage.
When your own pages legitimately frame your own content.
Sensitive, state-changing pages like settings or payments.
No — it's bypassable; rely on the headers.

questions.q: What is a WAF and what does it protect against?

answer.oneLiner: A Web Application Firewall inspects HTTP traffic at the edge and blocks common attack patterns (SQLi, XSS, bots); it's defense-in-depth, not a replacement for secure code.
keyPoints: Sits in front (Azure Front Door / App Gateway, Cloudflare)
Rule-based: OWASP CRS, rate limits, geo/IP filters
Buys time against zero-days and known patterns
Complements, never replaces, app-level fixes
redFlags: Treating a WAF as full protection

dialogue.text: What is a WAF and what does it do for you?
A Web Application Firewall sits at the edge and inspects HTTP traffic, blocking known attack patterns like SQLi, XSS, and bad bots.
So can we skip fixing the code if we have one?
No — it's defense-in-depth that buys time against known patterns and zero-days, but it never replaces secure code.
An attacker abuses a legit checkout endpoint to drain a coupon — does the WAF catch it?
No — that's business-logic abuse with valid-looking requests; WAFs catch malicious patterns, not flawed logic, so I'd fix the rule server-side.

followUpsQA.q: Can a WAF stop business-logic abuse?
followUpsQA.a: No — valid-looking requests pass; logic flaws must be fixed in the app.

followUpsQA.q: What is the OWASP Core Rule Set?
followUpsQA.a: A standard set of generic WAF rules covering common attack classes.
At the edge — Azure Front Door/App Gateway, Cloudflare, etc.
Then it logs attacks but doesn't block them — prevention mode is the point.

questions.q: How do you handle input validation in a Web API?

answer.oneLiner: Validate every input on the server with an allow-list mindset; client validation is only UX, never a security control.
keyPoints: `[ApiController]` auto-validates `ModelState`
Use data annotations or FluentValidation
Allow-list expected formats/ranges, reject the rest
Validate even for authenticated/internal callers

dialogue.text: How do you handle input validation in a Web API?
Validate everything on the server with an allow-list mindset — define expected formats and ranges and reject anything else.
What does ASP.NET give you out of the box?
`[ApiController]` auto-checks `ModelState`, and I layer data annotations or FluentValidation on top.
The caller is an authenticated internal service — can you trust its payload?
No — I still validate; internal callers get compromised or buggy too, and validation is cheap insurance.

followUpsQA.a: It's only UX — anyone can bypass it with a direct request.
Allow-list accepts only known-good; deny-list always misses new bad inputs.
The `[ApiController]` attribute returns 400 on invalid models automatically.
Yes — auth proves identity, not that the payload is well-formed.

questions.q: Walk me through the OWASP Top 10 at a high level.

answer.oneLiner: It's OWASP's list of the most critical web app risks, currently led by Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, and Injection.
keyPoints: Broken Access Control (#1), Crypto Failures, Injection
Insecure Design, Security Misconfiguration
Vulnerable Components, Auth Failures, SSRF
It's awareness/prioritization, not a checklist of everything
redFlags: Naming only injection/XSS and nothing about access control

dialogue.text: Give me a high-level tour of the OWASP Top 10.
It's OWASP's prioritized list of the most critical web risks, currently led by Broken Access Control, then Cryptographic Failures and Injection.
Why is access control number one?
Because it's the most common and impactful — users reaching data or actions they shouldn't, like IDOR or missing role checks.
One entry is SSRF — what is it and why did it make the list?
Server-Side Request Forgery: the app fetches a URL an attacker controls, often hitting internal services or cloud metadata; it's common in modern cloud apps.

followUpsQA.a: Broken Access Control — most prevalent and high-impact (users acting beyond their permissions).
Tricking the server into fetching attacker-chosen URLs, often internal/metadata endpoints.

followUpsQA.q: Is the Top 10 a complete checklist?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's awareness and prioritization, not exhaustive coverage.
Still top-tier but below access control and crypto failures in the current list.

questions.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you're allowed to do.
keyPoints: AuthN = identity (login, tokens)
AuthZ = permissions (roles, policies, claims)
AuthN always happens first
ASP.NET: `UseAuthentication()` before `UseAuthorization()`

dialogue.text: Authentication vs authorization — what's the difference?
Authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you're allowed to do.
Which happens first in ASP.NET Core, and how is that wired?
Authentication first — `UseAuthentication()` must come before `UseAuthorization()` so the user is known before we check permissions.
If someone presents a valid token but hits an admin-only endpoint, which one fails?
Authentication passes — we know who they are — but authorization fails and returns 403, versus a 401 for no/invalid identity.

followUpsQA.q: Which middleware comes first and why?
followUpsQA.a: `UseAuthentication` before `UseAuthorization` — you must know the user before checking permissions.
AuthN: logging in with a password. AuthZ: only admins can delete users.
401 = not authenticated; 403 = authenticated but not allowed.
No — you need an identity to evaluate permissions against.

questions.q: What's the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect?

answer.oneLiner: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that issues access tokens for API access; OpenID Connect is an identity layer on top that adds an ID token to authenticate the user.
keyPoints: OAuth → access token (authorization / 'can call API')
OIDC → ID token (authentication / 'who the user is')
ID token is a JWT with user claims (`sub`, `email`)
Use OIDC when you need to log a user in, OAuth for delegated API access
redFlags: Using an access token to identify the user

dialogue.text: OAuth 2.0 versus OpenID Connect — clear it up for me.
OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that issues access tokens for calling APIs; OIDC is an identity layer on top that adds an ID token to authenticate the user.
So what's actually in each token?
The access token grants API access; the ID token is a JWT describing who the user is — claims like `sub` and `email`.
A junior uses the access token to populate the user's profile name — what's wrong?
Access tokens are for authorization, not identity — they may be opaque or lack user claims; the ID token (or userinfo endpoint) is the right source.

followUpsQA.q: What's in an ID token vs an access token?
followUpsQA.a: ID token = who the user is (claims); access token = permission to call an API.

followUpsQA.q: When do you only need OAuth?
followUpsQA.a: Delegated API access where you don't need to identify the end user.
No — that's OIDC; OAuth alone is authorization.

followUpsQA.q: What format is an ID token?
followUpsQA.a: A JWT with user identity claims.

questions.q: What is the PKCE flow and why is it used for SPAs and mobile apps?

answer.oneLiner: PKCE is the Authorization Code flow plus a one-time code verifier/challenge, so public clients that can't keep a secret can still exchange codes securely.
keyPoints: Client sends a hashed `code_challenge`, later proves it with `code_verifier`
Stops authorization-code interception/replay
Required for public clients (SPA, mobile, native)
No client secret needed — replaces the old implicit flow
redFlags: Still using implicit flow for SPAs
Embedding a client secret in a SPA

dialogue.text: What is PKCE and why does it exist?
It's the Authorization Code flow plus a one-time `code_verifier`/`code_challenge`, so public clients that can't keep a secret can still exchange codes securely.
Walk me through how the challenge protects the exchange.
The client sends a hashed `code_challenge` up front, then proves possession with the original `code_verifier` at token exchange — an intercepted code is useless without it.
Why not just embed a client secret in the SPA instead?
A SPA's bundle is public, so any 'secret' is extractable; PKCE replaces the secret with a per-request proof, which is why implicit flow was deprecated.

followUpsQA.q: Why was the implicit flow deprecated?
followUpsQA.a: It returned tokens in the URL fragment with no proof-of-possession — leaky and replayable.
In the client's memory/session for the duration of the flow, never sent until exchange.
Public clients — SPAs, mobile, native apps that can't store a secret.

followUpsQA.q: Does PKCE need a client secret?
followUpsQA.a: No — the verifier replaces it.

questions.q: Describe the structure of a JWT and how you validate one.

answer.oneLiner: A JWT has three base64url parts — header, payload (claims), and signature; you validate the signature, issuer, audience, and expiry before trusting it.
keyPoints: Header (alg) . Payload (claims) . Signature
Payload is encoded, not encrypted — never put secrets in it
Validate signature, `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `nbf`
Verify against the IdP's signing keys (JWKS)

code.note: Disabling any of these validations is a common, serious bug.

dialogue.text: Describe a JWT's structure.
Three base64url parts: a header with the algorithm, a payload of claims, and a signature over the first two.
Is the payload safe to put a secret in?
No — it's encoded, not encrypted; anyone can decode it, so it must never hold secrets.
A token arrives with `alg: none` and no signature — do you accept it?
Never — that's the classic bypass; I validate signature, `iss`, `aud`, `exp`/`nbf` against the IdP's JWKS keys and reject unsigned tokens.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't you store secrets in the payload?
followUpsQA.a: It's base64-encoded, not encrypted — trivially readable by anyone.
The IdP's published set of public signing keys used to verify the JWT signature.
Signature, issuer, audience, and lifetime (`exp`/`nbf`).
It means unsigned — an attacker could forge any claims.

questions.q: JWT vs cookie authentication — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Cookies suit server-rendered apps and are CSRF-prone but XSS-safer when HttpOnly; JWTs suit APIs/SPAs/mobile and cross-domain calls but need careful storage.
keyPoints: Cookies: stateful-ish, auto-sent, HttpOnly, needs CSRF protection
JWT: stateless, sent in Authorization header, good cross-domain
JWT downside: hard to revoke; cookie downside: CSRF
Hybrid: short-lived JWT + refresh token in HttpOnly cookie
redFlags: Storing JWTs in localStorage without considering XSS
Long-lived JWTs with no revocation plan

dialogue.text: JWT versus cookie authentication — when each?
Cookies fit server-rendered apps and are XSS-safer when HttpOnly but CSRF-prone; JWTs fit APIs/SPAs/mobile and cross-domain calls but need careful storage.
Where should a SPA actually store a JWT, then?
Ideally not in `localStorage` due to XSS — an HttpOnly cookie or in-memory with a refresh token is safer.
You need cross-domain API calls and easy revocation — what do you reach for?
The hybrid: a short-lived JWT access token for the API plus a refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie, rotated each use, so I get statelessness and a revocation point.

followUpsQA.q: Where should a SPA store a JWT?
followUpsQA.a: Not `localStorage` — prefer HttpOnly cookie or in-memory to limit XSS theft.

followUpsQA.q: How does the hybrid pattern work?
followUpsQA.a: Short-lived JWT access token + refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie, rotated on use.
CSRF — needs antiforgery tokens / SameSite.
Hard to revoke before expiry.

questions.q: How do refresh tokens work and where should they be stored?

answer.oneLiner: A refresh token is a long-lived credential exchanged for new short-lived access tokens; store it securely server-side or in an HttpOnly cookie and rotate it on every use.
keyPoints: Lets access tokens stay short-lived (minutes)
Rotate on each use; detect reuse → revoke the family
Store HttpOnly+Secure cookie (web) or secure storage (mobile)
Never put refresh tokens in localStorage

dialogue.text: How do refresh tokens work?
A refresh token is a long-lived credential you exchange for new short-lived access tokens, so access tokens can expire in minutes.
Where do you store one safely?
Server-side or in an HttpOnly+Secure cookie for web, secure storage on mobile — never in `localStorage`.
How would you catch a refresh token that's been stolen and replayed?
Rotation with reuse detection: each refresh issues a new token and invalidates the old; if an already-used token reappears, I revoke the whole token family.

followUpsQA.a: Issuing a new refresh token on each use and invalidating the previous one.

followUpsQA.q: How do you detect a stolen refresh token?
followUpsQA.a: Reuse detection — a replayed old token signals theft, so revoke the family.
HttpOnly+Secure cookie (web) or platform secure storage (mobile).
They limit the window if one leaks, since revocation is hard.

questions.q: Compare RBAC, claims-based, and policy-based authorization in ASP.NET Core.

answer.oneLiner: RBAC checks roles, claims-based checks specific user claims, and policy-based wraps both (plus custom logic) in named, reusable rules — policy-based is the most flexible.
keyPoints: RBAC: `[Authorize(Roles="Admin")]` — coarse-grained
Claims: assert a specific claim value (e.g. department)
Policy: named rules, combine claims + custom `IAuthorizationRequirement`
Prefer policies for anything beyond simple roles

code.note: Roles are really just claims under the hood.

dialogue.text: Compare RBAC, claims-based, and policy-based authorization.
RBAC checks roles, claims-based checks specific claim values, and policy-based wraps both plus custom logic into named, reusable rules.
Which do you default to and why?
Policy-based — it's the most flexible and centralizes the rule, so I'm not scattering role-string checks everywhere.
You need 'manager of the same department as the record' — which approach?
A custom policy with an `IAuthorizationRequirement` and handler, because that's resource-and-relationship logic roles or single claims can't express.

followUpsQA.q: When would you write a custom requirement handler?
followUpsQA.a: For logic beyond roles/claims — e.g. resource ownership or relational checks.
Named, reusable, centralized rules instead of scattered hardcoded role strings.
Yes — roles are essentially a kind of claim under the hood.
It's coarse-grained — fine for simple admin/user, weak for nuanced rules.

questions.q: What are Azure AD / Entra ID and Managed Identity, and how do they help?

answer.oneLiner: Entra ID (Azure AD) is Microsoft's cloud identity provider for users and apps; Managed Identity gives an Azure resource its own Entra identity so it can call services with no stored credentials.
keyPoints: Entra ID = IdP for OAuth/OIDC sign-in and tokens
Managed Identity = passwordless identity for app/VM/service
Platform rotates the credential — no secrets in code
Use it to access Key Vault, SQL, Storage securely
redFlags: Storing service principal secrets when MI is available

dialogue.text: What are Entra ID and Managed Identity?
Entra ID, formerly Azure AD, is Microsoft's cloud identity provider for users and apps; Managed Identity gives an Azure resource its own Entra identity.
What problem does Managed Identity solve?
It removes stored credentials — the platform issues and rotates the identity, so the app calls Key Vault, SQL, or Storage with no secret in code.
System-assigned or user-assigned — when would you pick user-assigned?
User-assigned when several resources should share one identity or it must outlive a single resource; system-assigned is tied to and dies with one resource.

followUpsQA.a: System is bound to one resource's lifecycle; user-assigned is standalone and shareable.
The platform issues and rotates tokens, so no credential lives in code or config.
Any Entra-protected service via RBAC — Key Vault, SQL, Storage, etc.
Microsoft's cloud IdP (formerly Azure AD) for OAuth/OIDC sign-in and tokens.

questions.q: Give an overview of ASP.NET Core Identity.

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core Identity is the built-in membership system for managing users, passwords, roles, and sign-in, typically backed by EF Core.
keyPoints: Handles registration, login, password hashing, lockout
Stores users/roles/claims (EF Core by default)
Supports 2FA, external logins, email confirmation
Good for first-party local accounts; use an IdP for SSO/B2C
redFlags: Rolling your own user/password store unnecessarily

dialogue.text: Give me an overview of ASP.NET Core Identity.
It's the built-in membership system for users, passwords, roles, and sign-in, backed by EF Core by default.
What does it handle for you out of the box?
Registration, login, password hashing, lockout, 2FA, external logins, and email confirmation.
When would you skip it for an external IdP?
When I need SSO across apps, social/enterprise federation, or B2C scale — then Entra ID or another OIDC provider beats managing local accounts myself.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use an external IdP instead?
followUpsQA.a: For SSO, federation, or B2C scale rather than first-party local accounts.
With a salted, adaptive hash (PBKDF2) — you don't roll your own.
EF Core by default for users, roles, and claims.
Yes — plus external logins and email confirmation.

questions.q: How do you revoke JWTs given they're stateless?

answer.oneLiner: Since JWTs are self-contained you can't truly invalidate one; you mitigate with short lifetimes plus a server-side denylist or token-version check.
keyPoints: Keep access tokens short-lived; revoke at the refresh step
Maintain a denylist/jti cache for forced logout
Bump a per-user 'token version'/security stamp to invalidate all
Rotate signing keys for a global kill-switch
redFlags: Claiming you can instantly revoke a stateless JWT
Hour-long access tokens with no revocation

dialogue.text: JWTs are stateless — how do you revoke one?
You can't truly invalidate a self-contained token, so I keep access tokens short-lived and revoke at the refresh step.
What if you need an immediate forced logout?
A server-side denylist keyed on `jti`, or bumping a per-user token version / security stamp so existing tokens fail validation.
Trade-off of a denylist versus just short expiry?
A denylist gives instant revocation but reintroduces server state and a lookup on every request; short expiry stays stateless but leaves a small valid window.

followUpsQA.a: A per-user value that, when changed, invalidates all previously issued tokens.

followUpsQA.q: Trade-off of a denylist vs short expiry?
followUpsQA.a: Denylist = instant revoke but stateful lookups; short expiry = stateless but a small valid window.
At the refresh step — stop issuing new access tokens.
Rotate the signing key so every existing token fails verification.

questions.q: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and where is each used?

answer.oneLiner: Symmetric uses one shared key (fast, for bulk data); asymmetric uses a public/private key pair (slower, for key exchange and signatures).
keyPoints: Symmetric: AES — fast, bulk data at rest/in transit
Asymmetric: RSA/ECC — key exchange, signing, certs
TLS uses both: asymmetric to agree a symmetric session key
Symmetric pain point is securely sharing the key
redFlags: Saying asymmetric replaces symmetric for bulk data

dialogue.text: Symmetric vs asymmetric encryption — and where each fits?
Symmetric uses one shared key and is fast for bulk data; asymmetric uses a public/private pair and is slower, used for key exchange and signatures.
TLS — which does it use?
Both: asymmetric to authenticate and agree on a shared key, then symmetric for the actual session traffic because it's fast.
Why not just encrypt everything with RSA and skip symmetric?
RSA is far slower and size-limited per operation; it's impractical for bulk data, which is exactly why TLS hands off to a symmetric key.

followUpsQA.a: Asymmetric to safely agree a key, symmetric for fast bulk encryption of the session.

followUpsQA.q: Why not encrypt everything with RSA?
followUpsQA.a: It's slow and size-limited — unworkable for large payloads.
Securely distributing the shared key.
AES (symmetric); RSA/ECC (asymmetric).

questions.q: What's the difference between hashing and encryption?

answer.oneLiner: Encryption is reversible with a key; hashing is a one-way fingerprint with no way back.
keyPoints: Encryption: two-way, needs a key (confidentiality)
Hashing: one-way, fixed length (integrity, passwords)
Hashing isn't encryption and shouldn't be reversible
Encoding (base64) is neither — not security
redFlags: Calling hashing 'encryption you can't undo' loosely

dialogue.text: Hashing versus encryption?
Encryption is reversible with a key; hashing is a one-way fingerprint you can't reverse.
When do you use each?
Encryption for confidentiality of data you must read back; hashing for integrity checks and storing passwords.
A dev says base64 'encrypts' the payload — your reaction?
Base64 is encoding, not security — it's trivially reversible with no key, so it provides no confidentiality at all.

followUpsQA.a: Encryption for reversible confidentiality; hashing for integrity and passwords.
No — it's reversible encoding, not encryption.
No — one-way is the whole point.

followUpsQA.q: How is hashing used for passwords?
followUpsQA.a: Store a salted slow hash, compare hashes on login.

questions.q: How should you store user passwords?

answer.oneLiner: Never store them in plaintext or with a fast hash — use a slow, salted, adaptive algorithm like bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2.
keyPoints: Unique random salt per password (stored with the hash)
Use bcrypt / PBKDF2 / Argon2, not MD5/SHA-256 alone
Slow + adaptive work factor resists brute force
ASP.NET Core Identity does this for you

dialogue.text: How should you store user passwords?
Never plaintext and never a fast hash — use a slow, salted, adaptive algorithm like bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2.
Why is a fast hash like SHA-256 a bad choice here?
It's too fast — attackers can try billions of guesses per second; adaptive hashes add a tunable work factor to slow that down.
Two users pick the same password — why won't the stored hashes match?
Because each gets a unique random salt, so identical passwords hash differently, defeating precomputed rainbow tables.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a fast hash like SHA-256 bad here?
followUpsQA.a: Speed lets attackers brute-force billions of guesses per second.
Rainbow-table attacks and identical-password hash collisions.
bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2 — slow and adaptive.

followUpsQA.q: Do you need to build this yourself?
followUpsQA.a: No — ASP.NET Core Identity handles it.

questions.q: How do you manage application secrets and connection strings?

answer.oneLiner: Keep secrets out of source control — use Azure Key Vault (accessed via Managed Identity) in production and user-secrets locally, injecting them at runtime.
keyPoints: Never commit secrets; no secrets in appsettings or repo
Prod: Key Vault + Managed Identity (no stored creds)
Local dev: `dotnet user-secrets`
CI/CD: secret variables / secure store, masked in logs

code.note: DefaultAzureCredential uses Managed Identity in Azure, dev creds locally.

redFlags: Secrets in plaintext appsettings in prod

dialogue.text: How do you manage secrets and connection strings?
Keep them out of source control — Azure Key Vault via Managed Identity in production, and `dotnet user-secrets` locally, injected at runtime.
How does the app read Key Vault without its own credential?
`DefaultAzureCredential` uses the Managed Identity in Azure and developer creds locally, so there's no stored secret to fetch the secrets.
How do you stop secrets leaking into CI/CD logs?
Use the platform's secret variables / secure store and mark them masked, and never echo them — the runner redacts them from output.

followUpsQA.q: How do you avoid secrets in CI logs?
followUpsQA.a: Use masked secret variables and never echo them; the runner redacts them.
The need for any stored credential to authenticate to Azure services.
`dotnet user-secrets`, outside the repo.
Uses Managed Identity in Azure, dev credentials locally.

questions.q: What's the difference between protecting data at rest and in transit?

answer.oneLiner: In transit means encrypting data moving over the network (TLS/HTTPS); at rest means encrypting stored data (disk/DB/blob).
keyPoints: In transit: TLS 1.2+/HTTPS, mTLS internally
At rest: TDE, disk/storage encryption, column encryption
You need both — they cover different threats
Manage keys via KMS/Key Vault
redFlags: Assuming HTTPS covers stored data too

dialogue.text: Data at rest versus in transit — distinguish them.
In transit is data moving over the network, protected by TLS/HTTPS; at rest is stored data, protected by disk, DB, or column encryption.
Do you really need both?
Yes — they cover different threats; TLS won't help if someone steals a backup, and disk encryption won't help an attacker sniffing the wire.
Does at-rest encryption stop SQL injection?
No — the database decrypts transparently for legitimate queries, so an injection still reads plaintext; at-rest protects stolen media, not query-level attacks.

followUpsQA.q: Does at-rest encryption stop SQL injection?
followUpsQA.a: No — the DB serves decrypted data to valid queries; injection still reads it.
Transparent Data Encryption — encrypts DB files at rest without app changes.
TLS 1.2+/HTTPS, plus mTLS internally.
In a KMS / Key Vault.

questions.q: How do you isolate data in a multi-tenant application?

answer.oneLiner: Pick an isolation model on a cost-vs-isolation spectrum — separate database per tenant, separate schema, or a shared schema with a tenant discriminator.
keyPoints: DB-per-tenant: strongest isolation, costlier to scale
Schema-per-tenant: middle ground
Shared schema + TenantId: cheapest, must filter every query
Enforce the tenant filter centrally (EF global query filter) — never per-query trust

code.note: A missed WHERE TenantId is a cross-tenant data leak.

redFlags: Relying on developers to remember the tenant filter

dialogue.text: How do you isolate data in a multi-tenant app?
Pick a model on the cost-vs-isolation spectrum: database per tenant, schema per tenant, or a shared schema with a TenantId discriminator.
You go shared-schema — what's the big risk?
A single missing `WHERE TenantId` leaks one tenant's data to another, so I enforce the filter centrally, not per query.
How do you keep that filter from being forgotten or spoofed?
An EF global query filter applies it everywhere automatically, and I set the current tenant server-side from the auth context — never from a client-supplied value.

followUpsQA.a: A forgotten tenant filter cross-leaks data between tenants.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you set the current tenant?
followUpsQA.a: Server-side from the authenticated context, never from client input.

followUpsQA.q: How do you enforce the filter centrally?
followUpsQA.a: EF Core global query filter so every query is scoped automatically.
Database-per-tenant — but costliest to scale.

questions.q: What does handling PII and GDPR basics involve?

answer.oneLiner: Collect the minimum personal data, secure it, and honor rights like access and erasure; GDPR requires a lawful basis and breach notification.
keyPoints: Data minimization + purpose limitation
Encrypt PII, restrict access, log who reads it
Support right to access / erasure (delete or anonymize)
Lawful basis (e.g. consent) + ~72h breach notification
redFlags: No way to delete a user's data

dialogue.text: What does handling PII and GDPR basics involve?
Collect the minimum personal data with a lawful basis, secure it, and honor user rights like access and erasure.
What's the 'right to be forgotten' in practice?
On request you delete or anonymize a user's personal data, which means your design needs a real way to erase it, not just hide it.
A breach exposes PII — what's the clock you're working against?
GDPR generally requires notifying the supervisory authority within about 72 hours of becoming aware, so detection and an incident plan matter.

followUpsQA.q: What is the right to be forgotten?
followUpsQA.a: A user's right to have their personal data deleted or anonymized on request.
Pseudonymized can be re-linked with a key; anonymized cannot — only the latter leaves GDPR scope.
Roughly 72 hours to notify the authority after becoming aware.
Data minimization and purpose limitation — collect only what you need.

questions.q: What is the principle of least privilege and how do you apply it?

answer.oneLiner: Give every user, service, and process only the minimum access it needs to do its job — nothing more.
keyPoints: App DB account: only needed CRUD, not db_owner
Scope tokens/roles narrowly; deny by default
Managed Identity with tight RBAC over broad keys
Review and revoke unused access regularly
redFlags: Running everything as admin/sa for convenience

dialogue.text: Define least privilege and how you apply it.
Give every user, service, and process only the minimum access it needs — nothing more.
How does it look for a database account?
The app's DB user gets only the CRUD it needs on its tables — not `db_owner` or `sa` — so a compromise can't drop or read everything.
An app is compromised — how does least privilege change the outcome?
It shrinks the blast radius: a narrowly-scoped identity or token limits what the attacker can reach, instead of handing them the whole system.

followUpsQA.q: How does it limit blast radius?
followUpsQA.a: A compromised, narrowly-scoped identity can reach far less than a broad one.

followUpsQA.q: How does it apply to DB accounts?
followUpsQA.a: Grant only needed CRUD, never db_owner/sa.

followUpsQA.q: How does it pair with Managed Identity?
followUpsQA.a: Tight RBAC scopes beat broad shared keys.
Deny by default, grant explicitly, review and revoke unused access.

questions.q: How would you respond if you discovered a secret had been committed to the repository?

answer.oneLiner: Treat it as compromised: rotate the secret immediately, then remove it from history and add scanning to prevent recurrence.
keyPoints: Rotate/revoke the secret first — assume it's leaked
Move it to Key Vault / secret store
Purge from git history (and notify if it was pushed)
Add secret scanning + pre-commit hooks
redFlags: Just deleting the file without rotating
Assuming a private repo means it's safe

dialogue.text: You find an API key committed to the repo. First move?
Treat it as compromised and rotate or revoke it immediately — assume it's already leaked.
Why isn't deleting the file enough?
Because it still lives in git history and on any clone or fork, so the secret remains recoverable until it's rotated.
It was a private repo only the team can see — does that change anything?
No — I still rotate; private isn't a guarantee, history persists, and access can change, so the safe assumption is exposure, then purge history and add scanning.

followUpsQA.q: Why is deleting the file not enough?
followUpsQA.a: The secret stays in git history and clones until it's rotated.

followUpsQA.q: How do you prevent it next time?
followUpsQA.a: Add secret scanning and pre-commit hooks.
Rotate/revoke the secret — assume it's compromised.

followUpsQA.q: Where should the secret have lived?
followUpsQA.a: Key Vault or a secret store, never the repo.
